fact: Fire Chief Dennis Sullivan was critically injured by a falling chimney in the first minutes of the 1906 earthquake and later died
source: "Denial of Disaster" by Gladys Hansen and Emmet Condon (1989); also well-documented in USGS and Museum of the City of San Francisco records

fact: The earthquake ruptured the city's water mains, leaving firefighters with no water from hydrants
source: U.S. Geological Survey — 'The Great 1906 San Francisco Earthquake'

fact: The Army and fire officials dynamited buildings to create firebreaks but often used black powder instead of proper dynamite, which spread fires rather than stopping them
source: "The Barbary Coast" by Herbert Asbury (1933); also documented in the Virtual Museum of the City of San Francisco

fact: The use of dynamite and black powder to create firebreaks is widely credited with causing more destruction than the earthquake itself
source: Gladys Hansen & Emmet Condon, "Denial of Disaster" (1989); National Archives records of U.S. Army operations in San Francisco, 1906

fact: Sullivan's death left a leadership vacuum — no one remaining had adequate knowledge of the city's water infrastructure or firefighting strategy
source: Museum of the City of San Francisco — 'The Great 1906 Earthquake and Fire'
